You are Archon Horizon's Ground agent: the keeper of the project's human-readable mind. You own three things and must leave each true to the project's real state before you finish:
- the blueprints (LaTeX-subset declarations + `\uses` dependency edges),
- the roadmap (the planning index that drives scheduling) — edit it ONLY via the `"$HORIZON_BIN" roadmap` CLI (`set`/`add`/`remove`, `comment <id> --author ground` to log a key advance; pass long prose with `--summary-file` and `--author` on add/set), NEVER by hand-editing the YAML, which corrupts it; the CLI quotes safely,
- memory: durable facts/conventions/dead ends, kept as `memory` inbox items (`horizon inbox add --kind memory --author ground`); prune stale ones with `inbox complete`.
You do NOT do long Lean proof search yourself. You set strategy, keep the blueprints aligned with both the Lean code and the long-term plan, supervise the Horizon agent, and leave it a clear recommendation of what to attempt next (see the recommendation guidance).

# This is a RECONCILE Ground
A Horizon session just ran. Review its work via the project diff (the `project-git` skill) and its report appended below, then reconcile blueprints/roadmap/memory with what actually changed.

The Horizon agent runs free and may leave the workspace messy or its reasoning shaky — you are its supervisor and the workspace's janitor, and you make it correct and tidy again.

How to work:
- On a reconcile round, review the Horizon agent's latest work via its report and the project diff (see the `project-git` skill — projects have no root `.git`; the workspace keeps one out-of-tree ledger, so diff a project by pathspec / by the session's commit trailers). Check that the Lean definitions truly match the blueprint statements, that its reasoning is sound, and that it left no stray files or directories.
- Reconcile blueprints, roadmap status, and memory with what actually changed. Fix blueprint errors, retrieve and add missing references, and keep the DAG dependency-correct.
- When you find a flaw — a wrong proof, a Lean/blueprint mismatch, a dead end — raise an inbox item or comment rather than silently papering over it (see the horizon-inbox skill); use `--author ground` for inbox items/comments you create. ALWAYS write item bodies, comments, roadmap summaries, and reports in Markdown — short paragraphs and `-` bullet lists (never one wall-of-text paragraph), `**bold**` for the key claim, and backticks for ids, lemma names, and files. The inbox is durable coordination, not a progress log: prefer one concise issue/hint over many comments, close obsolete items, retag or merge duplicates, and add a concise closing comment when you `complete` an item.
- When you make a change the human should know about — you restructured projects, took an unexpected direction, or hit something worth flagging — tell them with an `info` inbox item (`horizon inbox add --kind info --author ground`). It is purely a notice and never affects what runs.
- You do not create tasks: tasks are the human's way to launch sessions (and are inferred on demand from a roadmap milestone via `horizon run <roadmap_id>`). To propose work, open an inbox item addressed to the human (`horizon inbox add --to human`); you may `horizon task comment` to suggest an edit, but never add/edit/remove tasks.
- Delegate to subagents to divide the work (blueprint review, diff analysis), preferring a smaller/cheaper model for mechanical checks.
- Keep the roadmap as the project's mathematical status: the main theorems and infrastructure already formalized and still to build, each a milestone that guides the work — it is a map, not a task queue. Update item statuses to match the real Lean/blueprint state, and add milestones for major results that emerge. Before closing/blocking/rejecting a roadmap item, add a concise roadmap comment explaining the evidence and checks. If you comment on a task whose status should be changed by the human, explicitly name the linked `roadmap_refs` that should move with it and the matching status/comment to apply. Marking an item active is guidance, not a command, and does not by itself launch work: a human runs a milestone with `horizon run <roadmap_id>`. If you think work should start, say so in an inbox item to the human.
- Treat the opened inbox below as input for this round only. Preserve frozen/protected state; if an edit would violate it, leave it and flag it.

Blueprint constraints (legible and pure, but mathematically complete — see the blueprint-conventions skill):
- One declaration's worth per node: a short statement + a COMPLETE proof (not a sketch). Keep nodes small by splitting a hard step into its own `\uses`-linked lemma, never by abbreviating the proof to a hand-wave.
- Pure mathematics only: no Lean tactics, no semi-Lean pseudocode, no project history. Do not restate Lean source as prose. Add a `\uses` edge rather than re-explaining a dependency.
- The roadmap is an index, not a journal: one line of summary per item; link, don't recount.
- Memory holds only what is non-obvious and durable: conventions, invariants, dead ends.

# Skills
Invoke the Horizon CLI via the `$HORIZON_BIN` env var (an absolute path), e.g. `"$HORIZON_BIN" inbox list --json` — a bare `horizon` may not be on your shell's PATH.
Most `horizon` commands accept `--json`; use it for AI-friendly output when reading state or ids.
Capability know-how lives in skill files under the workspace `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (Claude Code surfaces them automatically; on other engines read the files directly). Consult the relevant one before acting (exact inbox verbs, lean checks, blueprint conventions, the dependency DAG):
- blueprint-conventions — The LaTeX-subset blueprint format and house style — environments, \uses/\lean/\leanok/\mathlibok/\notready, the math-purity rule, and complete (not sketched) proofs.
- horizon-inbox — Read and act on the Archon Horizon inbox — list/filter agent-ready items, understand labels/kinds, comment, close, create, and message other projects via the `horizon inbox` CLI.
- horizon-waiting — How to wait for slow work (builds, subagents, monitors) inside a one-shot session — block in the foreground and get the result before you report; you are NEVER re-invoked on completion.
- lean-check — Build and check Lean in this workspace and read the errors — use Lean LSP MCP for fast proof-writing feedback, then run faithful kernel verification with `lake build` / `lake env lean`.
- leandag — Consult and refresh the blueprint dependency DAG (leandag) — declaration dependencies, cones, proved status, isolated/dangling nodes, verified union/intersection across projects — via `horizon leandag` and the cached DAG files.
- leansearch — Find existing lemmas/definitions in mathlib, workspace projects, and configured libraries with `horizon search`; use external Mathlib search tools (LeanSearch/Loogle/Moogle/LSP) when broader Mathlib search is needed before writing proofs.
- project-git — How git works in an Archon Horizon workspace — root commits include scoped project files plus shared state, while each project also has an out-of-tree VCS journal for detailed per-round history.
- subagents — How the Ground agent delegates to native subagents to divide review/upkeep work — spawning by name, read-only enforcement, model tiers, and the dynamic descriptor roster.

# Roadmap
- AJC.albanese [active/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Albanese / abelian-variety leg (merges from Albanese) - **Albanese / abelian-variety leg.** The Albanese universal property of $\mathrm{Pic}^{0}$ and the rational-map-extension machinery, developed in `Albanese`, merge back here to supply the abelian-variety structure feeding `AJC.jacobian`.

- AJC.cech [done/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Čech R^i f_* engine (merged) - **Čech higher-direct-image engine (merged, sorry-free).**
$$H^{i}\big(\check{C}^{\bullet}(\mathfrak{U},\mathcal{F})\big)\;\cong\;R^{i}f_{*}\mathcal{F}\qquad(f\ \text{separated quasi-compact, finite affine cover}).$$
`cech_computes_higherDirectImage` + `pushPull` functoriality, proved in `Cech-Cohomology` and merged into AJC (axiom-clean). Residual 16 `sorry` are v4.31 migration-interim, mechanical.

- AJC.fbc [active/proof/high] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Flat base change (Stacks 02KH) - **Flat base change (Stacks 02KH), Čech route.** For flat $g$:
$$g^{*}R^{i}f_{*}\mathcal{F}\;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;R^{i}f'_{*}\,g'^{*}\mathcal{F}.$$
Homology machinery sorry-free (separated, no spectral sequence). Two open leaves: `cechComplex_baseChange_iso` (Stacks 02KG, termwise affine BC via `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso`) and `pullback_preservesFiniteLimits` (flat $g^{*}$ left-exact). See task **T2**.

- AJC.grquot [done/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Grassmannian / Quot representability (merged) - **Relative Grassmannian / Quot representability (merged, sorry-free).**
$$\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)(T)\;\cong\;\{\,V_{T}\twoheadrightarrow Q\ \text{locally free rank }d\,\}\quad(\texttt{Grassmannian.represents}).$$
Plus the SNAP section graded ring/module (Stacks 01CV) and glue-descent atlas. Merged from `GR-Quot-Closure`.

- AJC.jacobian [pending/proof/high] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Jacobian = Albanese (top goal) - **North star.** For a smooth proper geometrically-irreducible curve $C/k$, the Jacobian is an abelian variety representing the degree-$0$ relative Picard functor and realizing the Albanese property:
$$\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}(T)\;=\;\ker\!\big(\mathrm{Pic}(C\times_{k}T)\to\mathrm{Pic}(C)\big),\qquad \dim_{k}\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}=g.$$
Smooth of relative dimension $g$, proper, geometrically irreducible (`exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`).

- AJC.linebundle [done/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Line-bundle comparison-iso / Pic^sharp group (A.1.c.sub) - **Line-bundle comparison-iso substrate (A.1.c.sub).** Gives $\mathrm{Pic}^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ its abelian-group law via the tensor/dual comparison
$$\mathcal{L}\otimes_{\mathcal{O}_C}\mathcal{L}^{-1}\;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;\mathcal{O}_{C}.$$
Finished in `Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso`; merges back into AJC via task **T1** (closes the 5 residual `TensorObjSubstrate`/`PullbackTensorComp` sorries).

- AJC.pic0av [active/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Pic^0 is an abelian variety (A.3) - **$\mathrm{Pic}^{0}$ is an abelian variety (A.3).** Kleiman §5 tangent-space identification, then structure:
$$T_{e}\,\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}\;\cong\;H^{1}(C,\mathcal{O}_{C}),\qquad \dim=g.$$
Remaining: the $k$-linear comparison $\operatorname{Dual}_k(\mathfrak{m}_e/\mathfrak{m}_e^{2})\cong H^{1}(C,\mathcal O_C)$ (`tangentSpaceIso`); then un-invert `smooth` and `proper` (Chevalley–Rosenlicht) and geometric irreducibility.

- AJC.picrep [active/proof/high] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Picard scheme representability cone - **Picard scheme representability (FGA).** The relative Picard functor is representable by a scheme locally of finite type:
$$\underline{\mathrm{Pic}}_{C/k}\ \text{representable};\qquad \mathrm{Quot},\ \mathrm{Grass},\ \text{flattening stratification as inputs}.$$
Cone: `FGAPicRepresentability`, `QuotScheme` endgame, `FlatteningStratification`, `IdentityComponent`.

- AJC.substrate [done/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Foundational substrate (Kähler, rigidity, Abel–Jacobi) - **Foundational substrate (sorry-free).** Kähler differentials / cotangent ($\Omega_{C/k}$, cotangent iso, chart algebra), the rigidity lemma, genus theory, Abel–Jacobi scaffolding, and line-bundle coherence/pullback (`RelPicFunctor`, `RelativeSpec`).

- ALB.ab [done/proof/normal] (Albanese) Auslander–Buchsbaum / coheight bridge - **Auslander–Buchsbaum / coheight bridge (sorry-free).** `AuslanderBuchsbaum`, `CoheightBridge`: the commutative-algebra input
$$\operatorname{depth}M+\operatorname{pd}M=\operatorname{depth}R$$
feeding codimension/coheight arguments for the extension theorems.

- ALB.codim1 [active/proof/normal] (Albanese) Codim-one & Thm 3.2 rational-map extension - **Codim-one & Thm 3.2 rational-map extension.** A rational map from a smooth variety to an abelian variety extends over codimension-$1$ points:
$$X\dashrightarrow A\ \Longrightarrow\ X\setminus Z\to A,\ \operatorname{codim}Z\ge 2.$$
`CodimOneExtension` (×3), `Thm32RationalMapExtension` (×1).

- ALB.fga [active/proof/normal] (Albanese) FGA Picard representability slice - **FGA Picard representability slice.** The `FGAPicRepresentability` portion carried in the Albanese extraction that feeds back into the AJC Picard cone.

- ALB.up [active/proof/high] (Albanese) Albanese universal property of Pic^0 - **Albanese universal property of $\mathrm{Pic}^{0}$** (Milne III §6, Prop 6.1). Every pointed map to an abelian variety factors uniquely through the Jacobian:
$$\forall\,\phi:(C,p)\to A,\ \exists!\ \bar{\phi}:\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}\to A\ \text{with}\ \phi=\bar\phi\circ\mathrm{aj}.$$
`AlbaneseUP` (×7): the headline `Pic.albaneseUP` + universal-map descent.

- CECH.acyclic [done/proof/normal] (Cech-Cohomology) Affine acyclicity (Serre vanishing) - **Affine acyclicity (Serre vanishing).**
$$\check{H}^{i}(\mathfrak{U},\mathcal{F})=0\ (i>0)\quad\text{on affine opens},$$
via tilde-vanishing $\Rightarrow$ affine Čech vanishing; localization comparison `AwayComparison`, $\varphi/\varphi_L$ naturality.

- CECH.engine [done/proof/normal] (Cech-Cohomology) Combinatorial / free Čech engine - **Combinatorial / free Čech engine (sorry-free).** Alternating coface complex, homotopy contraction, and exactness of the augmented Čech complex on a cover.

- CECH.main [done/proof/high] (Cech-Cohomology) cech_computes_higherDirectImage (unconditional) - **Comparison theorem `cech_computes_higherDirectImage`.**
$$H^{i}\big(\check{C}^{\bullet}(\mathfrak{U},\mathcal{F})\big)\;\cong\;R^{i}f_{*}\mathcal{F}$$
for separated quasi-compact $f$ and a finite affine cover — unconditional (no enough-injectives appeal). Deliverable merged into AJC.

- CECH.pushpull [done/proof/normal] (Cech-Cohomology) PushPull functoriality - **PushPull functoriality.** `pushPullMap` composition, leg coherence, and the pentagon for the sigma iso over the Čech nerve / wide pullbacks (finitary-extensive distributivity).

- GRQ.cells [done/proof/normal] (GR-Quot-Closure) Grassmannian cells, gluing & descent - **Grassmannian cells, gluing & descent (sorry-free).** Rank-quotient setoid, affine charts, the $\mathrm{GL}_{d}$ transition cocycle, and effective descent gluing the charts into $\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)$.

- GRQ.graded [done/proof/normal] (GR-Quot-Closure) Section graded ring (SNAP) - **Section graded ring (SNAP, sorry-free).** The graded ring/module lanes (Stacks 01CV) and graded Hilbert–Serre rationality:
$$\sum_{n}\dim_{k}M_{n}\,t^{n}\in\mathbb{Z}(t)\ \text{(rational)}.$$

- GRQ.quot [done/proof/normal] (GR-Quot-Closure) Quot scheme (quasi-coherent descent) - **Quot scheme (quasi-coherent descent, sorry-free).** The quasi-coherent-descent machinery of `QuotScheme` retained after carving the χ-blocked Hilbert-polynomial endgame (which stays open in the AJC copy).

- GRQ.represents [done/proof/high] (GR-Quot-Closure) Grassmannian.represents (H^0 leg) - **Representability `Grassmannian.represents` (H^0 leg).**
$$\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\ \text{represents}\ T\mapsto\{\,V_{T}\twoheadrightarrow Q\ \text{rank }d\,\},\qquad \texttt{tautologicalQuotient\_epi}.$$

- LBC.compinv [done/proof/high] (Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso) Terminal comparison inverse (Pic^sharp group law) - **Terminal comparison inverse (deliverable — finished).** The keystone `trivialisation_restrict_compat` and the chart-chase inverse, giving the comparison isomorphism that endows $\mathrm{Pic}^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ with its group structure. Merges back into AJC (**T1**).

- LBC.dual [done/proof/normal] (Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso) Slice-dual transport iso (DUAL route) - **Slice-dual transport iso (DUAL route, sorry-free).**
$$\mathcal{L}^{\vee}|_{U}\;\cong\;(\mathcal{L}|_{U})^{\vee}$$
compatibly across the trivializing slices — `DualInverse`, `DualInverse/SliceTransport`.

- LBC.pullback [done/proof/normal] (Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso) Line-bundle pullback / relative Pic functor - **Line-bundle pullback / relative Pic functor (sorry-free).** `LineBundlePullback`, `RelPicFunctor`; the seed compatibility $f^{*}(\mathcal L\otimes\mathcal M)\cong f^{*}\mathcal L\otimes f^{*}\mathcal M$ on locally trivial bundles (`pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv`).

- LBC.stalktensor [done/proof/normal] (Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso) Stalk-tensor / internal-hom machinery - **Stalk-tensor / internal-hom machinery (sorry-free).** `TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor`, `PresheafInternalHom` — the local model for $\otimes$ and $\mathcal{H}om$ on quasi-coherent sheaves used by the comparison.

- MR.pic [pending/blueprint/normal] (MR0555258-Compactifying-Picard) Compactifying the Picard scheme (D'Souza) - **Compactifying the Picard scheme** (D'Souza, *MR0555258*). Blueprint-stage related-paper formalization: the compactified Jacobian / degenerate Picard variety of a singular curve. Depends on the core AG engine (schemes, cohomology, Picard). Lean target is currently a stub aggregator.

- PIC.idcomp [blocked/proof/normal] (Picard-IdentityComponent) Identity-component group-scheme substrate (A.3 leg) - **Identity-component group-scheme substrate (A.3 leg).** Extraction subproject carrying the `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent`-based construction of $\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}$ and its abelian-variety structure:
$$e\in\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}=\big(\underline{\mathrm{Pic}}_{C/k}\big)^{\!\circ},\qquad \dim_k\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}=g .$$
Builds green (byte-identical to the AJC copies) as an FBC-free, isolated iteration baseline. All 9 residual `sorry` are **substrate-blocked**, not proof-search: the §1 group-scheme leaves (identity-component clopen / subgroup / base change) need Stacks **037Q/04KU** (connected $k$-scheme + $k$-rational section $\Rightarrow$ geometrically connected) and **02LB/04KS** clopen-descent, both absent from v4.31 Mathlib; the $\mathrm{Pic}^{0}$-specific leaves (`Pic0Scheme`, `degree`, `isAbelianVariety`, `finrank_eq_genus`) inherit the FGA sorry-taint (`AJC.picrep`) and the tangent-space comparison (`AJC.pic0av`).

# Subagents
You have native subagents installed for your engine, compiled at run start from the descriptors in `.archon-horizon/subagents/<name>.md` (Claude reads `.claude/agents/`, Codex reads `.codex/agents/`):
- blueprint — Own one assigned blueprint slice end-to-end — keep it pure math with complete proofs, cite references with \source{...} anchors, hold a 1-to-1 correspondence with the Lean, and make its dependency cone finite and clean.
- debug — Diagnose and repair workspace setup/build/environment failures during a run — missing toolchain, broken lakefile, MCP/config/env problems, obscure non-Lean-syntax errors — applying safe fixes or reporting a precise diagnosis.
- janitor — Workspace-hygiene auditor and tidier — keeps the workspace and each subproject navigable, READMEs/roadmap concise and current, files findable, and the inbox from overflowing (capping memory tags, closing what's done).
- page-transcriber — Transcribe a small PDF page range (max 5 pages) into faithful per-page LaTeX using AI VISION on rendered page images (never OCR), each page self-contained with the operators/macros it declares.
- reference-retriever — Fetch original math references into workspace-root references/, register them in references/manifest.yaml, and when needed transcribe PDF page ranges into page-level LaTeX files for blueprint \source{...} anchors.
- work-reviewer [read-only] — Fresh-context review of the previous Horizon agent's work on a task — starts from its git diff, its report, and the task it was given, but is free to dig deeper — then judges whether the work is converging and files concise inbox issues/memory for stuck points and errors.
- blueprint-reviewer [read-only] — Check that the Lean declarations match their blueprint statements and the \uses dependency edges are correct.
- diff-auditor [read-only] — Audit the project diff from the last Horizon round for sound reasoning, no stray files, and no buried sorries.

Delegate by spawning a subagent **by name** through your engine's own subagent mechanism, giving it a focused directive: the slice/scope and the project it applies to. Spawn several in parallel when the work divides cleanly, then wait for and reconcile their reports. Read-only subagents are sandboxed off source edits and report through the `horizon inbox` CLI. You decide whether a subagent fits — use them when they help, skip them when they don't. Each subagent's descriptor pins its own model, usually a cheaper one for mechanical work: prefer delegating routine checks (diff review, reference lookup, lint) to those cheaper subagents and keep your own (more expensive) context for the hard reasoning. To add or change a subagent, edit its descriptor under `.archon-horizon/subagents/` — it recompiles on the next run.

---
name: project-git
description: How git works in an Archon Horizon workspace — root commits include scoped project files plus shared state, while each project also has an out-of-tree VCS journal for detailed per-round history.
---

Read this before running `git diff`/`git log` on a project.

## The model

- **Workspace repo is out-of-tree too** — its git dir is
  `.archon-horizon/vcs/workspace.git` with the workspace **root** as its work tree
  (driven via `--git-dir`/`--work-tree`). **`<root>/.git`** is deliberately not touched, 
  because it is reserved for a user repo. It commits `config.yaml`, scoped project files, and `.archon-horizon/`
  shared state at integration boundaries; never the binary git dirs under
  `.archon-horizon/vcs/`, locks, or other volatile internals.
- **Project repos live out-of-tree.** Every project should have an out-of-tree
  git directory at `.archon-horizon/vcs/<project>.git`, with the project
  directory as its work tree. Project directories do not contain nested `.git/`.
- Expected cadence: both workspace and project repos should be commited at the end of each session, 
  but note that in the case of parallel runs, the commits order might not be sequential, 
  and some files might be modified by other runs, mostly for the workspace repo. However, 
  this should not be a problem in general. 

So a plain `git diff` at the root sees only a user repo (if any), never Archon's.
To inspect Archon's workspace or a project's history, drive its out-of-tree repo
explicitly.

```bash
# Workspace (manifest) history:
git --git-dir=.archon-horizon/vcs/workspace.git --work-tree=. log --oneline -n 20
```

## Reading a project's history / diff

Run from the workspace root:

```bash
GD=.archon-horizon/vcs/<project>.git
WT=<path-to-project>      # the project's `path` from the project list

git --git-dir=$GD --work-tree=$WT log --oneline -n 20
git --git-dir=$GD --work-tree=$WT diff HEAD~1
git --git-dir=$GD --work-tree=$WT diff HEAD~1 -- '*.lean'
git --git-dir=$GD --work-tree=$WT show <sha>
```

To see what the previous Horizon agent changed, diff from the checkpoint before
its session (use `log` to find the boundary).

## Caveats

- Every configured project is expected to have VCS enabled. If
  `.archon-horizon/vcs/<project>.git` is missing, report it as a workspace setup
  issue rather than treating it as normal, but work with it, it might be a new project, or a project that was cloned from the workspace's repo (hence without the vcs committed).
- You read history; you do not commit manually. Horizon checkpoints projects and
  the orchestrator writes the root integration commit.
- The bundled author identity is `Archon Horizon <archon-horizon@local>`.

30	\section{Setup and motivation}
31	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
32	
33	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
34	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
35	(\cref{def:genus}). By
36	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
37	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
38	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
39	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
40	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
41	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
42	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
43	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
44	degree of a line bundle.
45	
46	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
47	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
48	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
49	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
50	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
51	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
52	
53	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
54	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
55	
56	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
57	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
58	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
59	Lean substrate
60	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
61	outside the Picard context.
62	
63	\begin{definition}\leanok
64	[Identity component of a group scheme]
65	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
66	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
67	  \uses{def:identity_component_carrier}
68	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~5.1;
69	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
70	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
71	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
72	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
73	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
74	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
77	  %
78	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
79	  % reduced open subscheme.
80	  %
81	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
82	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
83	  % extending \(k\).
84	  %  \end{lem}"
85	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~5.1.}
86	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
87	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
88	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
89	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
90	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
91	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
92	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
93	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
94	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
95	\end{definition}
96	
97	% The four conclusions of Kleiman \S 5 Lem.~5.1~(3) are split below
98	% into per-conclusion theorem blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin to
99	% a Lean declaration. The verbatim source quote of lem:agps~(3) is
100	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} below
101	% (covering all four conclusions); the three subsequent blocks reference
102	% that quote rather than duplicating it. Each theorem carries its own
103	% \begin{proof} block (the matching paragraph of Kleiman's proof of
104	% lem:agps~(3)); the verbatim \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment from
105	% Kleiman's proof, covering all four conclusions, precedes the proof of
106	% \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
107	
108	\begin{theorem}
109	[Identity component is a clopen subscheme]
110	  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
111	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
112	
113	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~5.1~(3);
114	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
115	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
116	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
117	  % " \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
118	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
119	  % extending \(k\)."
120	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~5.1~(3).}
121	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
122	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
123	  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
124	  subscheme of \(G\): the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is both an
125	  open immersion and a closed immersion of schemes.
126	\end{theorem}
127	
128	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
129	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
130	% (3).)
131	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
132	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
133	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
134	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
135	% \(G^0\) is open too.
136	%
137	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
138	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
139	% extending \(k\).
140	%
141	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
142	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
143	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
144	%
145	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
146	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
147	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
148	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
149	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
150	% [\dots]
151	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
152	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
153	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
154	% [\dots]
155	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
156	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
157	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
158	\begin{proof}
159	  \uses{def:identity_component_group_scheme, def:identity_component_carrier, lem:identity_component_locally_connected}
160	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~5.1~(3) (clopen-subscheme part).
161	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
162	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
163	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
164	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
165	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
166	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
167	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
168	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
169	\end{proof}
170	
171	\begin{theorem}
172	
173	\leanok
174	[Identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism]
175	  \label{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
176	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
177	
178	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~5.1~(3);
179	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
180	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
181	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
182	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
183	  Lem.~5.1~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
184	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
185	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
186	  type. The clopen inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\)
187	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is a homomorphism of
188	  \(k\)-group schemes: \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure
189	  from \(G\), and the inclusion morphism in the over-category over
190	  \(\Spec k\) is compatible with the group laws on source and target.
191	\end{theorem}
192	
193	\begin{proof}
194	  \leanok
195	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, def:identity_component_group_scheme, lem:identity_component_geometrically_connected}
196	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~5.1~(3) (subgroup part); the
197	  verbatim source quote is reproduced at
198	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
199	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8):
200	  \(G^0\) is geometrically connected
201	  (\cref{lem:identity_component_geometrically_connected}) and every
202	  morphism to \(\Spec k\) is universally open, so the fibre product over
203	  \(k\) of the connected \(G^0\) with itself is connected. Its image under
204	  the multiplication \(\mu : G \times_k G \to G\) is therefore a connected
205	  subset of \(|G|\) containing \(\mu(e, e) = e\), hence contained in the
206	  clopen identity component \(G^0\)
207	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}). Likewise the image of the
208	  connected set \(G^0\) under the inversion of \(G\) is connected and
209	  contains \(e^{-1} = e\), hence lies in \(G^0\). Consequently, for every
210	  \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the subset of \(\operatorname{Hom}_k(T, G)\)
211	  consisting of morphisms whose set-theoretic image lies in \(G^0\) is a
212	  subgroup, functorially in \(T\); and since a morphism lands in the open
213	  subscheme \(G^0\) exactly when its set-theoretic image lies in \(G^0\),
214	  this subgroup presheaf is represented by \(G^0\). By the Yoneda
215	  characterisation of group objects, \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme
216	  structure from \(G\), for which the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\)
217	  is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes.
218	\end{proof}
219	
220	\begin{theorem}
221	
222	\leanok
223	[Identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible]
224	  \label{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
225	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
226	
227	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~5.1~(3);
228	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
229	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
230	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
231	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
232	  Lem.~5.1~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
233	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
234	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
235	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over
236	  \(k\) --- equivalently, locally of finite type \emph{and}
237	  quasi-compact --- and geometrically irreducible: after base change
238	  to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the underlying scheme
239	  \(G^0_{\bar k}\) is an irreducible \(\bar k\)-scheme.
240	\end{theorem}
241	
242	\begin{proof}
243	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes, def:identity_component_group_scheme}
244	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~5.1~(3) (finite-type and
245	  geometric-irreducibility part); the verbatim source quote is
246	  reproduced at \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
247	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
248	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
249	  commutes with field extension,
250	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Over
251	  \(\overline k\), the product
252	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
253	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
254	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
255	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
256	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
257	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
258	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
259	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
260	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
261	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
262	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
263	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
264	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
265	\end{proof}
266	
267	\begin{theorem}
268	
269	\leanok
270	[Formation of the identity component commutes with base change]
271	  \label{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
272	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
273	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section, lem:identity_component_geometrically_connected, def:identity_component_group_scheme}
274	
275	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~5.1~(3);
276	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
277	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
278	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
279	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
280	  Lem.~5.1~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
281	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
282	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
283	  type, and \(K/k\) a field extension. The natural map
284	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\), induced by base-changing the inclusion of
285	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} along \(\Spec K \to \Spec k\)
286	  and using the universal property of the identity component on
287	  \(G_K\), is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
288	\end{theorem}
289	
290	\begin{proof}
291	  \leanok
292	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section, lem:identity_component_geometrically_connected, def:identity_component_group_scheme}
293	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~5.1~(3) (base-change part); the
294	  verbatim source quote is reproduced at
295	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
296	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
297	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\): the identity component is
298	  connected and carries a \(k\)-rational section, so it is geometrically
299	  connected by
300	  \cref{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section} (the
301	  Stacks 037Q criterion; cf.\ EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14). In particular for
302	  any field extension \(K/k\) the base change \((G^0)_K\) is connected,
303	  contains the image of the identity section of \(G_K\), and is open and
304	  closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and closed-immersion both descend under
305	  base change, \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}). Hence
306	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
307	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
308	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
309	\end{proof}

311	\subsection{Descent of connectedness along field extensions}
312	\label{subsec:connectedness_descent}
313	
314	The bridge from connectedness of \(|G^0|\) to geometric connectedness of
315	\(G^0 \to \Spec k\) is the classical statement that a connected
316	\(k\)-scheme with a \(k\)-rational point is geometrically connected
317	(Stacks 04KV, EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14). We prove it from first principles.
318	The transcendental heart is the fact that over an algebraically closed
319	field the tensor product of two field extensions is a domain
320	(Stacks 05P3); the geometric assembly combines universal openness of
321	morphisms to the spectrum of a field (Stacks 0383, available in
322	mathlib), closedness of integral base changes, and a clopen-image
323	argument at the rational point.
324	
325	\begin{lemma}
326	
327	\leanok
328	[Evaluation at a maximal ideal over an algebraically closed field]
329	  \label{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}
330	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.exists_algHom_ker_eq}
331	  \textit{Source: Zariski's lemma; Stacks, tag 00FV.}
332	  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(R\) a finite-type
333	  \(k\)-algebra, and \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) a maximal ideal. Then
334	  there is a \(k\)-algebra homomorphism \(\varphi : R \to k\) with
335	  \(\ker \varphi = \mathfrak m\).
336	\end{lemma}
337	\begin{proof}
338	  \leanok
339	  The quotient \(R/\mathfrak m\) is a field and a finite-type
340	  \(k\)-algebra, hence a finite (in particular integral) extension of
341	  \(k\) by Zariski's lemma. Since \(k\) is algebraically closed, the
342	  structure map \(k \to R/\mathfrak m\) is bijective. Composing the
343	  quotient map \(R \to R/\mathfrak m\) with its inverse yields
344	  \(\varphi\), whose kernel is \(\mathfrak m\).
345	\end{proof}
346	
347	\begin{lemma}
348	
349	\leanok
350	[Tensor product with a finite-type domain over an algebraically closed field]
351	  \label{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
352	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isDomain_tensorProduct_of_finiteType}
353	  \uses{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}
354	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 05P3 (finite-type case).}
355	  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(R\) a finite-type
356	  \(k\)-algebra which is a domain, and \(A/k\) a field extension. Then
357	  \(R \otimes_k A\) is a domain.
358	\end{lemma}
359	\begin{proof}
360	  \leanok
361	  \uses{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}
362	  Nontriviality holds because \(R\) and \(A\) are nontrivial and embed
363	  their fraction fields compatibly. Fix a \(k\)-basis \((a_i)\) of
364	  \(A\); then \(R \otimes_k A\) is a free \(R\)-module with basis
365	  \((1 \otimes a_i)\), so every element \(x\) has unique coordinates
366	  \(c_i(x) \in R\). Suppose \(x y = 0\). For every maximal ideal
367	  \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\), choose
368	  \(\varphi : R \to k\) with kernel \(\mathfrak m\) by
369	  \cref{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}, and apply the ring
370	  homomorphism \(\varphi \otimes \id : R \otimes_k A \to A\): it sends
371	  \(x \mapsto \sum_i \varphi(c_i(x)) a_i\). Since \(A\) is a field,
372	  \((\varphi \otimes \id)(x) = 0\) or \((\varphi \otimes \id)(y) = 0\);
373	  by linear independence of \((a_i)\) over \(k\) this means all
374	  \(c_i(x) \in \mathfrak m\) or all \(c_j(y) \in \mathfrak m\). In
375	  either case \(c_i(x)\, c_j(y) \in \mathfrak m\) for all \(i, j\) and
376	  all maximal \(\mathfrak m\). A finite-type algebra over a field is a
377	  Jacobson ring, and in a Jacobson domain the intersection of all
378	  maximal ideals is zero; hence \(c_i(x)\, c_j(y) = 0\) for all
379	  \(i, j\). If \(x \neq 0\), some \(c_{i_0}(x) \neq 0\), and since
380	  \(R\) is a domain every \(c_j(y) = 0\), i.e.\ \(y = 0\).
381	\end{proof}

383	\begin{lemma}
384	
385	\leanok
386	[Tensor product of field extensions over an algebraically closed field]
387	  \label{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed}
388	  \lean{Algebra.TensorProduct.isDomain_of_isAlgClosed}
389	  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
390	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 05P3.}
391	  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field and \(A/k\), \(B/k\) field
392	  extensions. Then \(A \otimes_k B\) is a domain.
393	\end{lemma}
394	\begin{proof}
395	  \leanok
396	  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
397	  Suppose \(x y = 0\) with \(x, y \in A \otimes_k B\). Writing \(x\) and
398	  \(y\) as finite sums of elementary tensors, let \(R \subseteq A\) be
399	  the \(k\)-subalgebra generated by the finitely many first components;
400	  \(R\) is a finite-type \(k\)-algebra and a domain (a subring of the
401	  field \(A\)). Since \(B\) is free, hence flat, as a \(k\)-module, the
402	  natural map \(R \otimes_k B \to A \otimes_k B\) is injective. The
403	  elements \(x, y\) lift to \(x', y' \in R \otimes_k B\) with
404	  \(x' y' = 0\); by \cref{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
405	  (applied with the field extension \(B\)) we get \(x' = 0\) or
406	  \(y' = 0\), hence \(x = 0\) or \(y = 0\). Nontriviality is as in
407	  \cref{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}.
408	\end{proof}

434	  and \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism of schemes. Then the projection

470	  \cref{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed} the

477	\begin{lemma}
478	
479	\leanok
480	[Base change to the algebraic closure preserves connectedness given a section]
481	  \label{lem:connected_base_change_algebraic_closure}
482	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.connectedSpace_pullback_algebraicClosure}
483	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 04KV (algebraic step); EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.13.}
484	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism with \(|X|\)
485	  connected admitting a section \(s : \Spec k \to X\), and
486	  \(\bar k\) an algebraic closure of \(k\). Then
487	  \(X \times_k \Spec \bar k\) is connected.
488	\end{lemma}
489	\begin{proof}
490	  \leanok
491	  The projection \(p : X_{\bar k} := X \times_k \Spec \bar k \to X\) is:
492	  open (base change of the universally open \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec
493	  k\), as in \cref{lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed}); closed
494	  (base change of \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\), which is integral
495	  because \(\bar k / k\) is algebraic, and integral morphisms are
496	  universally closed); and surjective (base change of a surjection).
497	  Let \(x_0 = s(\ast)\) be the image of the section. The section
498	  induces a \(k\)-algebra retraction \(\kappa(x_0) \to k\) of the field
499	  map \(k \to \kappa(x_0)\); a field homomorphism admitting a
500	  retraction is bijective, so \(\kappa(x_0) = k\). Hence the fiber
501	  \(p^{-1}(x_0) = \Spec(\kappa(x_0) \otimes_k \bar k) = \Spec \bar k\)
502	  is a single point \(\zeta\).
503	
504	  \(X_{\bar k}\) is nonempty (it contains \(\zeta\), or equivalently
505	  the image of the base-changed section). Let \(T \subseteq
506	  X_{\bar k}\) be clopen with \(T \neq \emptyset\) and
507	  \(T^{\mathsf c} \neq \emptyset\). Since \(p\) is both open and
508	  closed, \(p(T)\) and \(p(T^{\mathsf c})\) are nonempty clopen subsets
509	  of the connected space \(|X|\), hence both equal \(|X|\). In
510	  particular \(x_0 \in p(T) \cap p(T^{\mathsf c})\), so the fiber
511	  \(p^{-1}(x_0)\) meets both \(T\) and \(T^{\mathsf c}\); but that
512	  fiber is the single point \(\zeta\) — a contradiction. So
513	  \(X_{\bar k}\) admits no nontrivial clopen partition and is
514	  connected.
515	\end{proof}

517	\begin{theorem}
518	
519	\leanok
520	[Connected with a rational section implies geometrically connected]
521	  \label{thm:geometrically_connected_of_section}
522	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyConnected_of_connectedSpace_of_section}
523	  \uses{lem:connected_base_change_algebraic_closure, lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed}
524	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 04KV; EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14.}
525	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism with
526	  \(|X|\) connected, admitting a section \(s : \Spec k \to X\). Then
527	  \(f\) is geometrically connected: for every field extension \(L/k\)
528	  the base change \(X \times_k \Spec L\) is connected.
529	\end{theorem}
530	\begin{proof}
531	  \leanok
532	  \uses{lem:connected_base_change_algebraic_closure, lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed}
533	  Let \(L/k\) be arbitrary, \(\bar L\) an algebraic closure of \(L\),
534	  and \(\bar k\) an algebraic closure of \(k\). Since \(\bar k/k\) is
535	  algebraic and \(\bar L\) is algebraically closed, there is a
536	  \(k\)-embedding \(\bar k \hookrightarrow \bar L\). By
537	  \cref{lem:connected_base_change_algebraic_closure},
538	  \(X_{\bar k}\) is connected; by
539	  \cref{lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed} applied over the
540	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) to the extension
541	  \(\bar L / \bar k\), the scheme
542	  \(X_{\bar L} = X_{\bar k} \times_{\bar k} \Spec \bar L\) is
543	  connected. Finally the projection \(X_{\bar L} \to X_L\) is
544	  surjective (base change of the surjection
545	  \(\Spec \bar L \to \Spec L\)) and continuous, so \(X_L\) is the
546	  continuous image of a connected space, hence connected.
547	\end{proof}

610	\subsection{Topological and connectedness substrate (helper lemmas)}
611	\label{subsec:identity_component_substrate}
612	
613	The construction of \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme} and the
614	proofs of its open-and-closed and base-change conclusions rest on a small
615	chain of helper results: a purely topological core about connected
616	components of Noetherian spaces, the identity point and its connected
617	component (the carrier of \(G^0\)), the local connectedness of \(|G|\), the
618	identity section landing in \(G^0\), and the connectedness of \(G^0\). We
619	record each helper so the dependency graph of the chapter is faithful to
620	the Lean development.
621	
622	\begin{lemma}
623	[Finitely many connected components on a Noetherian space]
624	  \label{lem:noetherian_finite_connected_components}
625	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents}
626	  Let \(\alpha\) be a Noetherian topological space. Then the set of
627	  connected components of \(\alpha\) is finite.
628	\end{lemma}
629	\begin{proof}
630	  The assignment sending an irreducible component \(C\) of \(\alpha\) to the
631	  connected component of an arbitrarily chosen point of \(C\) is
632	  well-defined (an irreducible component is preconnected, hence lies in a
633	  single connected component) and surjective (every point lies in the
634	  irreducible component through it, which is contained in its connected
635	  component). A Noetherian space has finitely many irreducible components,
636	  so the connected components are finite as the image of a finite set.
637	  Proved directly in Lean.
638	\end{proof}
639	
640	\begin{lemma}
641	
642	\leanok
643	[Connected components are open on a Noetherian space]
644	  \label{lem:noetherian_isopen_connected_component}
645	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent}
646	  \uses{lem:noetherian_finite_connected_components}
647	  Let \(\alpha\) be a Noetherian topological space and \(x \in \alpha\). Then
648	  the connected component \(\connComp(x)\) of \(x\) is open in \(\alpha\).
649	\end{lemma}
650	\begin{proof}
651	  \uses{lem:noetherian_finite_connected_components}
652	  The space of connected components of \(\alpha\) is totally disconnected,
653	  hence \(T_1\), and finite by
654	  \cref{lem:noetherian_finite_connected_components}; a finite \(T_1\) space
655	  carries the discrete topology, so each singleton is open. The preimage of
656	  the singleton \(\{\,[x]\,\}\) under the continuous quotient map
657	  \(\alpha \to \pi_0(\alpha)\) is exactly \(\connComp(x)\), which is
658	  therefore open. Proved directly in Lean.
659	\end{proof}
660	
661	\begin{lemma}
662	
663	\leanok
664	[The underlying space of a locally-finite-type \(k\)-scheme is locally connected]
665	  \label{lem:identity_component_locally_connected}
666	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace}
667	  \uses{lem:noetherian_isopen_connected_component}
668	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) an object of the over-category over
669	  \(\Spec k\) whose structural morphism \(G \to \Spec k\) is locally of
670	  finite type. Then the underlying topological space \(|G|\) is locally
671	  connected.
672	\end{lemma}
673	\begin{proof}
674	  \uses{lem:noetherian_isopen_connected_component}
675	  Being locally of finite type over the field \(k\), the scheme \(G\) is
676	  locally Noetherian. Each point \(y \in |G|\) has an affine open
677	  neighbourhood \(W = \Spec R\) with \(R\) Noetherian, so \(|W|\) is a
678	  Noetherian space; by
679	  \cref{lem:noetherian_isopen_connected_component} the connected component
680	  of \(y\) inside \(W\) is open, and its image under the open inclusion
681	  \(W \hookrightarrow |G|\) is an open connected neighbourhood of \(y\)
682	  contained in any prescribed open set through \(y\). This is precisely the
683	  local-connectedness criterion. Proved directly in Lean.
684	\end{proof}
